title: Memory — stack vs heap, the basics

phase1.output:
=== Stack vs Heap ===
Global variable: 42 (address: 0x100001000)
Stack variables:
localVar: 10 (address: 0x7ffffeeeee)
anotherLocal: 20 (address: 0x7ffffeeeea)

Heap variable:
*heapVar: 100 (address: 0x6000000000)

--- Key Differences ---
Stack: automatic, fast, limited size
Heap: manual, slower, larger size
phase1.explanation:
🎮 **Memory is where your game data lives.**

**Stack:**
• Automatic allocation (you don't delete it)
• Fast (just moves a pointer)
• Limited size (usually 1-8MB)
• Variables are local to functions
• Memory is freed when function returns

**Heap:**
• Manual allocation (`new` / `delete`)
• Slower (more overhead)
• Large size (can use most of RAM)
• Memory persists until you `delete` it

**Key differences:**

| Aspect | Stack | Heap |
|--------|-------|------|
| Allocation | Automatic | Manual (`new`) |
| Deallocation | Automatic (scope exit) | Manual (`delete`) |
| Speed | Fast | Slower |
| Size | Limited | Large |
| Lifetime | Function scope | Until deleted |

**Why this matters for games:**
• Player objects → often on heap (persist across frames)
• Temporary variables → stack (fast, short-lived)
• Large assets (textures, models) → heap

**PlusPlus-chan says:**
"Java-chan has a garbage collector. I have `new` and `delete`. You control memory. That's powerful — and dangerous. We'll learn to handle it."
phase1.openingDialogue: Memory. This is where C++ separates from the rest. 🧠💾

phase2.errorMessage:
42

(No error — but memory is LEAKED! The heap allocation is never freed.)
phase2.explanation:
You allocated memory on the heap with `new` but never freed it with `delete`.

**Memory leak!** The program ends, but the memory is still allocated. In a game, this would cause memory usage to grow over time.

**The fix:**
```cpp
int* ptr = new int(42);
std::cout << *ptr << std::endl;
delete ptr;  // ← Always delete heap memory!
```

**PlusPlus-chan says:**
"Every `new` needs a `delete`. Every `new[]` needs a `delete[]`. This is the #1 rule of manual memory management."

phase3.prompt:
Write a C++ program that:

1. Declares a local (stack) variable `stackVar` with value 5
2. Declares a global variable `globalVar` with value 10
3. Uses `new` to allocate an integer on the heap with value 15
4. Prints all three values and their addresses using `&`
5. Properly deletes the heap memory

Requirements:
- Use `#include <iostream>`
- Use `new` and `delete`
- Print addresses with `std::cout`
phase3.openingDialogue: Memory basics: know where your data lives. 🧠
